covered_sections: 1.3. Types of asexual reproduction>1.3.4. Vegetative reproduction
1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention>null

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why would a plant that is perfectly adapted to its environment benefit from reproducing asexually rather than sexually?

options.A: Asexual reproduction creates genetic variation for future environmental changes
options.B: Asexual reproduction allows the plant to produce offspring with the same successful traits without needing a mate
options.C: Asexual reproduction produces seeds that can disperse farther than sexual reproduction
options.D: Asexual reproduction requires more energy but ensures survival

questions.hint: If the parent is already successful, making identical copies preserves that success.
questions.explanation: In a stable environment where the parent is well-adapted, asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring that inherit the parent's successful traits, without requiring a mate or gamete formation. Option A is incorrect because asexual reproduction produces little variation.

source_trace.content_anchor: all the good characteristics of the parent are passed on to the offspring; offspring will grow in the same favourable environment as the parent

explanations_tiered.definition: Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring (clones), preserving successful traits in stable environments.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When the environment is stable, asexual reproduction ensures that well-adapted traits are passed intact without the unpredictability of genetic recombination.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a plant is already growing well, making exact copies means the new plants will also grow well, without needing another plant.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Asexual reproduction is advantageous in stable environments because it preserves the parent's successful genotype without requiring a mate or producing gametes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In stable, predictable environments, the fitness of the parent genotype is proven; sexual reproduction would introduce maladaptive recombination, while asexual propagation maintains optimal alleles.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that the parent is already successful in that environment.
Asexual reproduction creates clones identical to the parent.
Clones inherit the same successful traits.
misconceptions: Asexual reproduction does not increase genetic variation; that is a disadvantage, not a benefit.
Seeds are produced by sexual reproduction, not asexual (except apomixis, rare).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Asexual advantage in stable environment: clones preserve success.

glossary_entry.definition: Reproduction producing genetically identical offspring from one parent.

flashcard.front: Why is asexual reproduction good for a plant in a stable environment?
flashcard.back: It produces identical offspring with the parent's successful traits without needing a mate.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Asexual reproduction preserves successful traits; option A is false (little variation), C is false (seeds are sexual), D is false (less energy).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When is asexual reproduction most advantageous for a plant?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: In a stable environment where the parent's traits are already successful.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Reproduction from one parent producing identical offspring.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with sexual reproduction advantage in changing environments.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare advantages and disadvantages of asexual and sexual reproduction
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Asexual reproduction passes on successful traits without needing a mate; variation is not created (disadvantage in changing environments).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which hormone is primarily responsible for the development of secondary sexual characteristics in human males during puberty?
questions.hint: This hormone is produced by the testes and causes voice deepening and muscle growth.
questions.explanation: Testosterone, produced by Leydig cells in the testes, is the primary male sex hormone responsible for secondary sexual characteristics such as voice deepening, increased muscle mass, and body hair growth. Estrogen and progesterone are female hormones; FSH stimulates sperm production but does not directly cause secondary characteristics.

source_trace.content_anchor: testosterone is responsible for the growth and development of secondary sex characteristics in the male

explanations_tiered.definition: Testosterone is the primary androgen hormone responsible for male secondary sexual characteristics.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Testosterone promotes voice deepening (laryngeal growth), increased muscle mass, facial/body hair, and bone growth during male puberty.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Testosterone is the hormone that turns boys into men — it deepens the voice, builds muscles, and grows facial hair.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Testosterone, secreted by testicular Leydig cells, binds to androgen receptors, triggering the development of male secondary sexual characteristics at puberty.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Testosterone acts via the androgen receptor to upregulate gene expression for sex-specific traits; it is converted to dihydrotestosterone (DHT) in target tissues for stronger androgenic effects.
reasoning_steps: Recall that male secondary characteristics appear at puberty.
These changes are driven by testosterone from testes.
Estrogen drives female characteristics, FSH stimulates gamete production.
misconceptions: FSH does not cause secondary characteristics; it acts on seminiferous tubules for sperm production.
Testosterone is not the only hormone involved, but it is the primary one for male characteristics.

glossary_entry.definition: Male sex hormone responsible for secondary sexual characteristics.

flashcard.front: Which hormone causes male secondary sexual characteristics?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Testosterone is the male sex hormone; estrogen and progesterone are female; FSH regulates gametogenesis.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What hormone drives male puberty changes?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Male hormone causing secondary sexual traits.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that testosterone also regulates sex drive and sperm production.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate primary and secondary sexual characteristics
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Testosterone drives male secondary characteristics; estrogen is female; progesterone maintains pregnancy; FSH stimulates sperm/egg production.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where in the male reproductive system are sperm cells produced?
questions.hint: Sperm production occurs in tightly coiled tubes inside the testes.
questions.explanation: The seminiferous tubules within the testes are the sites of spermatogenesis (sperm production). The epididymis matures and stores sperm; the vas deferens transports sperm; the prostate gland secretes seminal fluid.

source_trace.content_anchor: Each testis is composed of numerous seminiferous tubules, in which the sperm develop

explanations_tiered.definition: Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules, which are coiled structures within the testes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Sertoli cells within the seminiferous tubules support developing sperm through meiosis and spermiogenesis.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Sperm are made inside tiny tubes in the testicles called seminiferous tubules.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The seminiferous tubules contain germ cells that undergo spermatogenesis; the epididymis then matures and stores the sperm.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Spermatogenesis takes approximately 64 days in the seminiferous tubules, from spermatogonia to spermatozoa, under the influence of FSH and testosterone.
reasoning_steps: Recall that sperm production is spermatogenesis.
Spermatogenesis occurs in seminiferous tubules of testes.
Epididymis = maturation/storage, vas deferens = transport, prostate = fluid secretion.
misconceptions: The epididymis does not produce sperm; it matures and stores them.
The testes have two compartments: seminiferous tubules (sperm production) and interstitial cells (testosterone).

glossary_entry.definition: Coiled tubes inside the testes where sperm are produced.

flashcard.front: Where in the male body are sperm made?
flashcard.back: In the seminiferous tubules of the testes.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Seminiferous tubules produce sperm; epididymis matures/stores; vas deferens transports; prostate secretes fluid.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: In the seminiferous tubules of the testes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Site of sperm production in the testes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use diagram of testis cross-section showing tubules.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify structures and functions of male reproductive organs
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Sperm are produced in seminiferous tubules; epididymis stores them; vas deferens transports them; prostate adds fluid.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the primary function of the uterus in the female reproductive system?

options.A: To house and nourish the developing embryo/fetus during pregnancy
options.C: To receive sperm during sexual intercourse
options.D: To secrete hormones that regulate the menstrual cycle

questions.hint: The uterus is where the baby grows during pregnancy.
questions.explanation: The uterus (womb) is a hollow, pear-shaped organ where the embryo implants and develops into a fetus. It provides physical protection and, via the placenta, nourishment. Option B is the ovary, option C is the vagina, option D is the ovary (estrogen/progesterone) and hypothalamus/pituitary.

source_trace.content_anchor: uterus, which is specialized for housing the embryo during its intrauterine existence

explanations_tiered.definition: The uterus is the organ where the embryo implants and develops into a fetus, providing protection and support.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The endometrium thickens each cycle to receive a blastocyst; if implantation occurs, the uterus expands dramatically to accommodate the growing fetus.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The uterus is the baby's home during pregnancy — it's where the fertilized egg grows into a baby.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The uterus provides a protected environment for embryonic and fetal development, with muscular walls for expansion and expulsion during labor.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The uterus is composed of three layers: perimetrium (outer), myometrium (smooth muscle for contractions), and endometrium (glandular lining for implantation).
reasoning_steps: Recall that the uterus is the 'womb'.
Its primary role is supporting pregnancy.
Egg production = ovary, sperm reception = vagina, hormone secretion = ovaries/pituitary.
misconceptions: The uterus does not produce eggs; the ovaries do.
The uterus does not secrete estrogen/progesterone; the ovaries and placenta do.

glossary_entry.definition: Organ where the embryo/fetus develops during pregnancy.

flashcard.front: What is the main function of the uterus?
flashcard.back: To house and nourish the developing baby during pregnancy.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Uterus houses the fetus; ovaries produce eggs; vagina receives sperm; ovaries secrete hormones.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Houses and nourishes the developing fetus.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify female reproductive structures and their functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Uterus houses the fetus; ovaries produce eggs; vagina receives sperm; hormones from ovaries.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which female reproductive structure is also known as the birth canal?
questions.hint: This structure extends from the cervix to the outside of the body.
questions.explanation: The vagina is the muscular canal that extends from the cervix to the body's exterior. It receives sperm during intercourse and serves as the birth canal during delivery. The cervix is the opening to the uterus; the oviduct carries eggs; the uterus houses the fetus.

source_trace.content_anchor: vagina, which extends from the cervix to the body's surface, is the organ of intercourse and the birth canal

explanations_tiered.definition: The vagina is the muscular canal that receives the penis during intercourse and serves as the passage for childbirth.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The vagina is a fibromuscular tube that connects the cervix to the external genitalia; it expands during labor to allow passage of the baby.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The vagina is the passageway that connects the uterus to the outside; babies come out through it during birth.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The vagina functions as the copulatory organ (receives sperm) and the birth canal (expels the fetus during parturition).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The vaginal wall has rugae (folds) that allow expansion; it is lined by stratified squamous epithelium that changes with estrogen levels.
reasoning_steps: Recall that 'birth canal' refers to the passage baby travels through.
This is the vagina.
Cervix is the opening of uterus, oviducts carry eggs, uterus holds fetus.
misconceptions: The cervix is not the birth canal; it is the opening at the bottom of the uterus that dilates during labor.
The uterus is the organ that holds the baby, not the canal it passes through.

glossary_entry.definition: Muscular canal that receives sperm and serves as the birth canal.

flashcard.front: What is the birth canal called?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The vagina is the birth canal; the cervix is the uterine opening; oviducts transport eggs; uterus houses the fetus.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Muscular canal; birth canal and copulatory organ.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Differentiate vagina (birth canal) from cervix (opening to uterus) and uterus (where baby grows).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify female reproductive structures and their functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The vagina is the birth canal; the cervix is the uterine opening; oviducts carry eggs; uterus houses the fetus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which a copper intrauterine device (IUD) prevents pregnancy?

options.A: It prevents fertilization by creating a toxic environment for sperm
options.B: It releases hormones that suppress ovulation
options.C: It blocks the cervix to prevent sperm entry

questions.hint: Copper IUDs create an environment that is toxic to sperm.
questions.explanation: Copper IUDs release copper ions that are toxic to sperm, preventing fertilization. They also alter the uterine lining to prevent implantation if fertilization occurs. Option B describes hormonal IUDs; option C describes diaphragms; option D describes tubal ligation.

source_trace.content_anchor: small T-shaped plastic and copper device . . . prevents implantation of a fertilized ovum

explanations_tiered.definition: A copper IUD is a T-shaped device placed in the uterus that releases copper ions toxic to sperm, preventing fertilization.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Copper ions impair sperm motility and viability, and also alter the endometrium to discourage implantation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The copper IUD creates a chemical environment in the uterus that stops sperm from swimming or surviving.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Copper IUDs release cupric ions that inhibit sperm motility and acrosome reaction, effectively preventing fertilization; they also cause endometrial inflammation incompatible with implantation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Copper IUDs produce a sterile inflammatory response in the endometrium; copper ions inhibit sperm motility by interfering with ion channels and ATP production.
reasoning_steps: Recall that copper IUDs are non-hormonal.
Copper ions are toxic to sperm.
Primary prevention is fertilization, not implantation (though implantation also prevented).
misconceptions: Copper IUDs do not release hormones; hormonal IUDs (IUS) release progesterone.
The IUD sits in the uterus, not the oviducts.

glossary_entry.definition: Intrauterine device releasing copper ions that impair sperm motility.

flashcard.front: How does a copper IUD work?
flashcard.back: Copper ions are toxic to sperm, preventing fertilization.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Copper IUDs create a toxic environment for sperm; hormonal IUDs suppress ovulation; diaphragms block the cervix; tubal ligation blocks oviducts.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the mechanism of a copper IUD?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Copper ions are toxic to sperm, preventing fertilization.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Device releasing copper ions that impair sperm.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Differentiate copper IUD (non-hormonal) from hormonal IUS (progesterone).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Copper IUDs are toxic to sperm; hormonal methods suppress ovulation; barrier methods block sperm entry; sterilization blocks tubes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the contraceptive implant and how does it work?

options.A: A small plastic tube inserted under the skin that slowly releases progesterone to prevent ovulation
options.B: A T-shaped device placed in the uterus that releases copper ions to kill sperm
options.C: A pill taken daily that contains estrogen and progesterone to suppress FSH and LH
options.D: A rubber sheath placed over the penis that traps sperm

questions.hint: The implant is placed in the arm and releases a hormone.
questions.explanation: The contraceptive implant is a small rod (about 4 cm) inserted under the skin of the upper arm. It slowly releases progesterone, which thickens cervical mucus and suppresses ovulation, lasting up to 3 years. Option B is an IUD, option C is the pill, option D is a condom.

source_trace.content_anchor: small plastic tube . . . inserted under the skin of the upper arm . . . slowly releases the hormone progesterone, preventing pregnancy

explanations_tiered.definition: The contraceptive implant is a subdermal rod that releases progestin to prevent pregnancy by inhibiting ovulation and thickening cervical mucus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Implant releases steady low-dose progesterone, suppressing LH surge (preventing ovulation) and making cervical mucus impenetrable to sperm.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A small rod placed under the skin of the arm releases a hormone that stops eggs from being released.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The etonogestrel implant inhibits ovulation by suppressing LH, thickens cervical mucus, and thins the endometrium, providing >99% efficacy for up to 3 years.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Progestin-only implants provide continuous negative feedback on the hypothalamus, reducing GnRH pulsatility and LH, blocking ovulation; they also induce an atrophic endometrium.
reasoning_steps: Recall implant is a hormone-releasing rod in the arm.
Hormone = progesterone (progestin).
Mechanism: prevents ovulation, thickens mucus.
misconceptions: Implant does not contain copper; that's the IUD.
Implant is not a pill; it's a long-acting reversible contraceptive (LARC).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Implant: progesterone rod in arm, stops ovulation.

glossary_entry.definition: Subdermal rod releasing progestin to prevent pregnancy.

flashcard.back: A small rod under the skin of the arm that releases progesterone to prevent ovulation.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Implant = subdermal progesterone rod; IUD is T-shaped in uterus; pill is oral; condom is barrier.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does the contraceptive implant work?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that implants are long-acting (3 years) and highly effective.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Implant is progesterone rod under skin; IUD is T-shaped in uterus; pill is daily oral; condom is barrier.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the function of a diaphragm in birth control?

options.A: To cover the cervix and prevent sperm from entering the uterus
options.B: To release hormones that prevent ovulation
options.C: To trap sperm inside the vagina after ejaculation
options.D: To block the oviducts so eggs cannot travel

questions.hint: The diaphragm is a barrier method placed inside the vagina.
questions.explanation: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped rubber cup that is inserted into the vagina before intercourse to cover the cervix, blocking sperm from entering the uterus. Option B describes hormonal methods, option C describes spermicides or condoms, option D describes tubal ligation.

source_trace.content_anchor: thin rubber disc, placed in the vagina before intercourse, covers the cervix and stops sperm entering the uterus

explanations_tiered.definition: The diaphragm is a barrier contraceptive that fits over the cervix to physically block sperm from entering the uterus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Used with spermicide for higher efficacy, the diaphragm prevents sperm from reaching the oviducts and fertilizing an egg.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The diaphragm is like a dome-shaped cover that blocks the entrance to the uterus so sperm can't get through.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The diaphragm is a cervical barrier; when correctly placed, it covers the external os, preventing sperm from ascending into the uterine cavity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The diaphragm must be fitted by a clinician; it works by mechanical occlusion of the cervical os and, when used with spermicide, provides chemical immobilization of sperm.
reasoning_steps: Recall that diaphragm is a barrier method.
It covers the cervix, blocking sperm entry.
Not hormonal, not trapping sperm, not tubal blocking.
misconceptions: The diaphragm does not release hormones; it is non-hormonal.
It must be inserted before intercourse and left in for several hours after.

glossary_entry.definition: Barrier contraceptive that covers the cervix to block sperm.

flashcard.back: It covers the cervix, blocking sperm from entering the uterus.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Diaphragm blocks the cervix; hormonal methods suppress ovulation; condoms trap sperm; tubal ligation blocks oviducts.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does a diaphragm prevent pregnancy?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It covers the cervix, blocking sperm from entering the uterus.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that diaphragms are often used with spermicide for better effectiveness.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Diaphragm blocks the cervix; hormonal methods prevent ovulation; condoms trap sperm; tubal ligation blocks oviducts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is tubal ligation (female sterilization)?

options.A: The cutting, tying, or blocking of the oviducts to prevent eggs from reaching the uterus
options.B: The removal of the ovaries to stop egg production
options.C: The insertion of a T-shaped device into the uterus to prevent implantation
options.D: The surgical removal of the uterus (hysterectomy)

questions.hint: This procedure blocks the tubes that carry eggs.
questions.explanation: Tubal ligation (laparotomy) is a surgical procedure where the oviducts are tied, cut, or blocked, preventing sperm from reaching eggs and eggs from traveling to the uterus. Option B is oophorectomy, option C is IUD insertion, option D is hysterectomy.

source_trace.content_anchor: woman may be sterilized by an operation in which her oviducts are tied, blocked or cut

explanations_tiered.definition: Tubal ligation is a permanent female sterilization procedure that occludes the fallopian tubes, preventing egg-sperm meeting.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The oviducts are blocked via clips, rings, or cautery; eggs are released but degenerate, and sperm cannot reach them.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Tubal ligation is a surgery that cuts or blocks the tubes eggs travel through, so they can't meet sperm.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Tubal ligation involves blocking the fallopian tubes, either by cutting, cauterizing, or applying clips, preventing fertilization by physically separating eggs and sperm.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Tubal occlusion is usually performed via laparoscopy; the blocked segment prevents ovum transport, and the ova are resorbed in the peritoneal cavity; menstrual cycles are unaffected.
reasoning_steps: Recall tubal ligation = 'tying tubes'.
Tubes = oviducts (fallopian tubes).
Prevents egg from meeting sperm.
misconceptions: Tubal ligation does not remove ovaries or uterus; hormone production continues.
Menstruation continues normally because the uterus and ovaries remain.

glossary_entry.definition: Permanent female sterilization by blocking the oviducts.

flashcard.back: Blocks or cuts the oviducts so eggs cannot reach sperm.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Tubal ligation blocks oviducts; oophorectomy removes ovaries; IUD is intrauterine device; hysterectomy removes uterus.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Surgical blocking of the oviducts for permanent female sterilization.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with vasectomy (male). Both are permanent but do not affect hormones.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Tubal ligation blocks oviducts; oophorectomy removes ovaries; IUD is a device; hysterectomy removes uterus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A woman using the fertility awareness method tracks her basal body temperature daily. She observes a sustained rise of 0.5°C after a period of lower readings. What does this temperature shift indicate?

options.D: The fertile window has just opened

questions.hint: Ovulation causes a small but sustained rise in basal temperature.
questions.explanation: After ovulation, progesterone from the corpus luteum raises basal body temperature by about 0.5°C. This sustained rise indicates that ovulation has occurred and the fertile window has closed. Option A is incorrect; option C requires additional signs; option D is incorrect (fertile window ends after ovulation).

source_trace.content_anchor: During or soon after ovulation, a woman's temperature rises by about 0.5°C

explanations_tiered.definition: Basal body temperature rises after ovulation due to progesterone, indicating the fertile period has ended.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Progesterone secreted by the corpus luteum is thermogenic, raising BBT by 0.3-0.6°C after ovulation; three consecutive days of elevated temperature confirm ovulation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: After the egg is released, the body temperature goes up a little bit and stays up — that's how you know ovulation happened.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A sustained BBT rise of at least 0.5°C for three consecutive days indicates that ovulation has occurred and the woman is no longer fertile in that cycle.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Progesterone acts on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center, increasing the set point; a biphasic BBT chart (lower follicular phase, higher luteal phase) confirms ovulation timing.
reasoning_steps: Recall that progesterone is released after ovulation.
Progesterone raises body temperature.
A sustained rise indicates ovulation has occurred.
misconceptions: BBT rise does not predict ovulation; it confirms it has already occurred.
The temperature rise is small and requires a special basal thermometer to detect.

glossary_entry.definition: Lowest body temperature at rest; rises after ovulation due to progesterone.

flashcard.front: What does a sustained BBT rise indicate?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Progesterone from corpus luteum raises BBT after ovulation; menstruation follows if no pregnancy.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does a rise in basal body temperature indicate?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Body temperature at rest; rises after ovulation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that BBT method confirms ovulation after the fact, so it is used to identify infertile days after ovulation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss natural methods of family planning
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Progesterone raises BBT after ovulation; temperature rise confirms ovulation has occurred.
